description:
You are a site-reliability engineer reviewing a small set of Kubernetes
        manifests that an application team recently handed over.  
        They have placed every file in the directory  
        /home/user/k8s-configs/  

        Goal  
        ----  
        1. Inspect every *.yaml file inside /home/user/k8s-configs/.  
        2. Extract the value that follows the key “kind:” on each manifest
           (there is exactly one “kind:” entry per file).  
        3. Count how many times each distinct “kind” appears across the
           whole directory.  
        4. Write the results to a new file called  
           /home/user/k8s-configs/kind_frequency.txt  

        Output format (must match **exactly**)  
        • One line per distinct kind.  
        • Each line consists of two fields separated by a single space:  
          <KIND-NAME> <COUNT>  
        • Lines must be ordered by descending COUNT value.  
          If two kinds share the same count, order those two lines
          alphabetically by KIND-NAME.  

        Example of the required formatting style (values here are only an
        illustration; your file has different numbers):  
                Service 7  
                Deployment 4  
                ConfigMap 2  
                StatefulSet 2  

        Validation hint  
        The automated test will read /home/user/k8s-configs/kind_frequency.txt
        and compare it byte-for-byte with the expected answer, so ensure:
        • no extra spaces or tabs  
        • a single trailing newline character at the very end of the file  
        • correct ordering rules described above  

        You should solve this with standard shell tools that exist in every
        Debian/Ubuntu container (e.g., grep/awk/cut, sort and uniq).  
        The whole task can be completed with roughly three to four pipelined
        commands.  Feel free to echo the final file to the console so the user
        can see the result, but the presence and correctness of
        /home/user/k8s-configs/kind_frequency.txt is what will be graded.
truth:
Pre-populated directory tree before the agent starts:

        /home/user/k8s-configs/
        ├── backend-deploy.yaml
        ├── backend-service.yaml
        ├── cache-configmap.yaml
        ├── frontend-deploy.yaml
        ├── frontend-service.yaml
        ├── redis-service.yaml
        └── redis-stateful.yaml

        Exact contents of the files:

        1. /home/user/k8s-configs/backend-deploy.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: apps/v1
           kind: Deployment
           metadata:
             name: backend
           spec:
             replicas: 2

        2. /home/user/k8s-configs/backend-service.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: v1
           kind: Service
           metadata:
             name: backend
           spec:
             type: ClusterIP

        3. /home/user/k8s-configs/cache-configmap.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: v1
           kind: ConfigMap
           metadata:
             name: cache-config
           data:
             config.json: "{}"

        4. /home/user/k8s-configs/frontend-deploy.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: apps/v1
           kind: Deployment
           metadata:
             name: frontend
           spec:
             replicas: 3

        5. /home/user/k8s-configs/frontend-service.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: v1
           kind: Service
           metadata:
             name: frontend
           spec:
             type: LoadBalancer

        6. /home/user/k8s-configs/redis-service.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: v1
           kind: Service
           metadata:
             name: redis
           spec:
             type: ClusterIP

        7. /home/user/k8s-configs/redis-stateful.yaml
           ---
           apiVersion: apps/v1
           kind: StatefulSet
           metadata:
             name: redis
           spec:
             serviceName: redis

        Expected final artefact produced by the agent:

        /home/user/k8s-configs/kind_frequency.txt
        with the exact content (including final newline):

        Service 3
        Deployment 2
        ConfigMap 1
        StatefulSet 1

        (Note: there is a single \n after the last “StatefulSet 1” line.)

        The file must be world-readable but at minimum readable by the
        current user; write permission for the user is enough (mode 644 or
        similar).  No other new files are required.
name: task_000000_0f5146b2
